title: IGA-ODIL: Optimizing DIscretre robust Loss with Isogeometric Analysis to solve forward and inverse problems faster using machine learning tools
authors: Maciej Paszyński, Tomasz Służalec
abstract: Physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) formulate the solution of partial differential equations as residual minimization problems over neural network parameterizations. Although highly flexible, optimization of PINNs using modern variants of Stochastic Gradient Descent algorithms is expensive. On the other hand, iterative computation of PINN parameterization using the Gauss-Newton method suffers from convergence difficulties, dense Jacobian structures, and poor conditioning that limit the effectiveness of second-order optimization methods. In this work, we introduce IGA-ODIL, a spline-based residual minimization framework combining ideas from Optimizing DIscrete Loss (ODIL), robust variational residual minimization, and Isogeometric Analysis (IGA). Instead of neural-network parameterizations of PINNs, the unknown solution is represented by smooth B-spline basis functions, leading to sparse structured Jacobians and efficient Gauss--Newton optimization. We also derive robust residual formulations based on weighted Gram operators, making the loss function related with the true error. The resulting systems inherit locality, sparsity, and approximation-theoretic properties of classical finite element and isogeometric methods while preserving the residual-learning philosophy of scientific machine learning. The proposed methodology is evaluated on several benchmark problems, including Poisson equations, convection-dominated advection--diffusion equations, Helmholtz problems with highly oscillatory solutions, nonlinear Allen--Cahn equations, and inverse Helmholtz parameter identification. Numerical experiments demonstrate orders-of-magnitude speedups compared with PINNs and CRVPINNs while maintaining high accuracy and robustness.

infographic.key_insight: IGA-ODIL replaces neural-network PINN parameterizations with smooth B-spline/isogeometric bases, yielding sparse Gauss--Newton optimization and faster, more stable residual minimization.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach offers a practical surrogate-solver framework for forward and inverse PDE problems where accuracy, conditioning, and runtime matter in computational engineering workflows.
method: Represent the unknown field with B-spline basis functions from Isogeometric Analysis (IGA).
Minimize a robust residual loss derived from ODIL and weighted Gram operators.
Exploit sparse Jacobians and Gauss--Newton iterations for efficient optimization.
infographic.evidence_signal: The method was benchmarked on Poisson, advection--diffusion, Helmholtz, Allen--Cahn, and inverse Helmholtz problems, showing orders-of-magnitude speedups over PINNs and CRVPINNs while preserving high accuracy.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on careful spline discretization, problem conditioning, and implementation of the Gauss--Newton solver for stiff or highly oscillatory PDEs.
infographic.paper_title: IGA-ODIL: Optimizing DIscretre robust Loss with Isogeometric Analysis to solve forward and inverse problems faster using machine learning tools

title: LEIA: Learned Environment for Interactive Architected Materials
authors: Haiqian Yang, Yuan Cao, Markus J. Buehler
abstract: World models have enabled interactive exploration of game environments and robotic manipulation, but physical engineering remains beyond their reach: real materials exhibit nonlinear constitutive laws, carry history-dependent internal state, undergo inertial dynamics, and may possess hierarchical structures spanning multiple length scales. We present LEIA (Learned Environment for Interactive Architected materials), a world model that lets engineers apply boundary conditions step by step and observe the resulting deformation and stress fields in real time. LEIA handles large three-dimensional unstructured meshes and generates autoregressive responses to user-specified loading. We introduce MicroPlate, a benchmark of architected plates spanning two regimes of microstructure modeling: architected lattices that resolve microstructure explicitly through three-dimensional geometry, and a homogeneous plate where microstructural change is modeled implicitly through internal degrees of freedom. MicroPlate is used to assess LEIA alongside four baseline methods across both regimes. Finally, we demonstrate that LEIA enables efficient candidate generation and ranking for fast surrogate-guided search for de novo designs of architected materials, with stress-accurate candidate ranking validated by finite element ground truth.

infographic.key_insight: LEIA is a learned world model for solid mechanics that updates deformation and stress fields step by step under applied boundary conditions, even for large 3D architected materials.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It offers real-time surrogate analysis and candidate ranking for architected material design, helping accelerate FEM-guided discovery and optimization.
method: Autoregressive world model for interactive loading and response prediction
Handles both explicit lattice microstructures and implicit homogeneous models with internal state
Works on large 3D unstructured meshes with nonlinear, history-dependent behavior
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper evaluates LEIA on MicroPlate, a benchmark spanning two microstructure regimes, and compares it against four baseline methods with finite-element ground truth for stress-accurate ranking.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on benchmark-specific training data and careful handling of mesh complexity, history effects, and extrapolation to unseen loading or designs.
infographic.paper_title: LEIA: Learned Environment for Interactive Architected Materials

title: High-Fidelity Industrial Crash Dynamics Prediction via Geometry-Aware Operator Learning with Memory-Efficient Low-Rank Attention
authors: Deepak Akhare, Mohammad Amin Nabian, Corey Adams, Sudeep Chavare, Sanjay Choudhry
abstract: Automotive crashworthiness optimization remains a safety-critical challenge, requiring the management of large-scale nonlinear structural deformations and energy dissipation through iterative, high-fidelity simulations. While traditional finite element solvers are computationally prohibitive, emerging operator learning frameworks provide rapid surrogate predictions; however, applying them to industrial-scale crash analysis, where complex geometry, contact nonlinearities, and rapidly evolving transient deformation coexist, remains an open challenge. In this paper, we demonstrate that the GeoTransolver framework provides a viable solution for accurate, high-fidelity crash dynamics prediction at industrial scale. Benchmarked on complex bumper beam and full-vehicle crash datasets, GeoTransolver captures multi-scale geometric context and accurately resolves plastic deformation patterns as well as acceleration profiles at critical occupant locations. Beyond the architecture itself, we propose and systematically evaluate a suite of temporal prediction recipes, including one-shot, time-conditional, and autoregressive rollout strategies, demonstrating that the one-shot approach achieves state-of-the-art accuracy with significantly reduced training overhead and inference latency. As a secondary contribution, we introduce a Fast Low-rank Attention Routing Engine (FLARE)-based modification to the GeoTransolver attention backbone that reduces memory overhead by approximately 2x while further improving predictive accuracy for O(N) long-range, high-frequency transients, preserving the geometry-aware cross-attention strengths of the base framework. Our results highlight the practical viability of geometry-aware operator learning for high-fidelity surrogate modeling of complex, safety-critical automotive dynamics.

infographic.key_insight: Geometry-aware operator learning can accurately predict industrial crash dynamics while capturing both deformation and acceleration responses with much lower cost than full finite element simulation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables faster crashworthiness design iteration for safety-critical automotive structures, supporting surrogate-based optimization on complex nonlinear transient problems.
method: Use GeoTransolver to learn crash dynamics from complex bumper beam and full-vehicle geometries.
Compare one-shot, time-conditional, and autoregressive temporal prediction recipes.
Add FLARE low-rank attention to cut memory use while preserving long-range geometric interactions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The approach is benchmarked on complex bumper beam and full-vehicle crash datasets, with one-shot prediction reported as state-of-the-art and FLARE improving accuracy while reducing memory by about 2x.
infographic.watch_item: Performance may depend on the quality and diversity of training crash datasets, and industrial deployment will need validation across unseen geometries, materials, and contact conditions.
infographic.paper_title: High-Fidelity Industrial Crash Dynamics Prediction via Geometry-Aware Operator Learning with Memory-Efficient Low-Rank Attention

title: PINNsur: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for PDEs on Curved Surfaces
authors: Pranav Jain, Navami Kairanda, Peter Yichen Chen, Oded Stein
abstract: Partial differential equations (PDEs) on surfaces are fundamental to scientific computing and geometry processing. A popular approach to solving PDEs on surfaces is the finite element method (FEM), where the surface is divided into discrete geometric elements (usually triangles). Recently, physics-informed neural networks (PINNs) have emerged as a continuous, mesh-free alternative that does not suffer from FEM's sensitivity to mesh quality or geometric discretization errors. We present PINNSur, a simple framework for using PINNs on curved surfaces: we train a neural field to approximate the surface's normals, and then we express surface differential operators using their projection from $\mathbb{R}^3$ onto the surface. Since every orientable manifold has well-defined normals, our method is suitable for all such surfaces, regardless of curvature or topology, enabling many geometry processing applications. Moreover, despite their empirical success in solving PDEs in flat Euclidean domains, PINNs lack convergence guarantees to the true solution of the underlying PDE, and there is limited systematic experimental evidence demonstrating such convergence. This gap restricts their adoption as reliable solvers compared to established methods like FEM, where convergence to the true solution is well understood and theoretically grounded. These surface PDEs are particularly challenging to solve convergently, as one must not only deal with the convergence of the function approximation, but also with the convergence of the geometric approximation of the surface itself. In this work, we empirically investigate the convergence behavior of PINNs for solving surface PDEs by introducing a simple empirical convergence test.

infographic.key_insight: PINNSur extends physics-informed neural networks to curved surfaces by learning surface normals and projecting 3D differential operators onto the manifold, making PINNs usable for broad classes of orientable surfaces.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This offers a mesh-free alternative to FEM for surface PDEs, potentially reducing sensitivity to mesh quality and geometric discretization in geometry processing and scientific computing.
method: Train a neural field to approximate the surface normal vector field.
Define surface differential operators by projecting Euclidean operators from R^3 onto the surface.
Use the framework to solve PDEs directly on curved, orientable manifolds.
infographic.evidence_signal: The paper evaluates convergence behavior with a simple empirical convergence test, focusing on whether PINNs can approach the true surface PDE solution as both function and geometry approximations improve.
infographic.watch_item: Reliable performance depends on accurately learning normals and geometric approximations, so convergence may be limited by surface representation errors as well as the usual PINN optimization challenges.
infographic.paper_title: PINNsur: Physics-Informed Neural Networks for PDEs on Curved Surfaces

title: Traceable Knowledge Graph Reasoning Enables LLM-Assisted Decision Support for Industrial VOCs in the Steel Industry
authors: Changqing Su, Yu Ding, Zuhong Lin, Hongyu Liu, Xi He, Zheng Zeng
abstract: Key knowledge for steel-industry volatile organic compounds (VOCs) governance is scattered across unstructured scientific literature, making it difficult to integrate process, pollutant, and control-technology evidence and increasing the risk of hallucination when general large language models (LLMs) answer low-frequency industrial questions. Here we developed Chat-ISV, a knowledge graph (KG) enhanced multi-agent Q&A system that parses a curated steel-industry VOCs literature corpus, constructs a Neo4j KG with 27180 nodes and 81779 semantic edges, and combines prompt-constrained extraction, chunk-centered topology optimization, multi-agent routing, source-backtracking retrieval, local literature retrieval, open-domain knowledge access, and interactive subgraph visualization. Benchmark tests and 400 expert blind evaluations showed that topology optimization reduced isolated nodes from 57% to 4.08% and that Chat-ISV achieved high factual reliability, with 96.93% precision, 72.63% recall, an F1-score of 0.830, and a mean score of 1.69/2.00. By converting fragmented environmental-engineering literature into traceable, queryable, and decision-support-oriented knowledge, Chat-ISV establishes a scalable environmental-informatics paradigm for reliable LLM deployment and intelligent pollution-control decision support in specialized industrial domains.

infographic.key_insight: Chat-ISV turns scattered steel-industry VOC knowledge into a traceable knowledge graph so LLMs can answer specialized pollution-control questions with less hallucination.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework supports reliable decision support for industrial emissions management by linking process, pollutant, and control-technology evidence in a queryable environment.
method: Parsed a curated VOC literature corpus and built a Neo4j knowledge graph with 27,180 nodes and 81,779 semantic edges.
Used prompt-constrained extraction, chunk-centered topology optimization, and multi-agent routing for retrieval and answer generation.
Added source-backtracking, local literature retrieval, open-domain knowledge access, and interactive subgraph visualization.
infographic.evidence_signal: Benchmark tests and 400 expert blind evaluations reported 96.93% precision, 72.63% recall, F1 = 0.830, and a mean score of 1.69/2.00; topology optimization reduced isolated nodes from 57% to 4.08%.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on high-quality curated literature and graph construction, so coverage gaps or extraction errors could still affect answer reliability.
infographic.paper_title: Traceable Knowledge Graph Reasoning Enables LLM-Assisted Decision Support for Industrial VOCs in the Steel Industry

title: Low-Magnification SEM May Suffice: Interpretable Deep Learning for Multi-Scale Fracture-Cause Classification in Zirconia-Toughened Alumina
authors: Julian Schmid, Pawel Astankow, Tom Vater, Julius Beck, Robert Cichon, Danny Krautz
abstract: Reliable identification of fracture origins in alumina matrix composite hip and knee implants is critical for quality assurance and patient safety, yet current fractographic workflows are time-consuming, partly subjective, and reliant on high-magnification scanning electron microscopy (SEM). We present an interpretable vision-transformer (ViT) workflow for automated classification of fracture causes in an alumina matrix composite (BIOLOX delta, CeramTec GmbH) widely used in total joint replacements. A dataset of 8,493 SEM images (50x-10,000x) was curated from five years of in-production burst and proof tests and annotated into three defect categories defined along the manufacturing chain: green body, hard machining, and material defects. Under severe class imbalance, the fine-tuned ViT reached an accuracy of 0.907 and a macro-F1 of 0.888 in stratified five-fold cross-validation, with a two-stage perceptual-hash/SSIM leakage audit confirming negligible specimen overlap. Notably, performance at low magnification (50x) was comparable to that at high magnification (1k-10kx), indicating that macro-scale features - mirror geometry and hackle line fields - already encode sufficient diagnostic signal. Grad-CAM attributions consistently localised on canonical fractographic cues (mirrors, hackles, pores, machining marks), aligning with established fractographic criteria. Together, these results position interpretable ViTs as a complementary tool for ceramic-implant quality assurance, enabling low-magnification pre-screening and reducing reliance on time-intensive high-magnification inspection.

infographic.key_insight: An interpretable vision transformer can classify fracture causes in zirconia-toughened alumina from SEM images, and low-magnification views can be nearly as informative as high-magnification ones.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables faster, less subjective implant fracture QA by using low-magnification SEM as an effective pre-screening step for ceramic quality control.
method: Fine-tuned ViT on 8,493 SEM images spanning 50x to 10,000x magnification.
Classified fractures into three manufacturing-related defect classes: green body, hard machining, and material defects.
Used Grad-CAM plus leakage auditing to check interpretability and specimen independence.
infographic.evidence_signal: In stratified five-fold cross-validation, the model achieved 0.907 accuracy and 0.888 macro-F1, with comparable performance at 50x and at 1k-10kx magnification.
infographic.watch_item: The workflow was validated on a specific ceramic implant material and manufacturing test dataset, so broader deployment will need external validation across other scanners, labs, and failure modes.
infographic.paper_title: Low-Magnification SEM May Suffice: Interpretable Deep Learning for Multi-Scale Fracture-Cause Classification in Zirconia-Toughened Alumina

title: VFEAgent: A Multimodal Agent Framework for End-to-End Automated Finite Element Analysis
authors: Jiachen Zhang, Junyi Lao, Chenghao Liu, Siyuan Liu, Shixin Wu, Linsen Zhang
abstract: Finite Element Analysis (FEA) serves as the cornerstone of modern engineering design. However, its workflow is inherently complex and relies heavily on domain expertise. Although recent efforts have integrated Large Language Models (LLMs) into FEA, existing approaches face limitations in handling multimodal inputs and executing complex tasks. To address these limitations, we propose VFEAgent, an end-to-end multi-agent system designed to automate FEA modeling and simulation directly from input images and problem descriptions. Our methodology integrates two core components: (1) a multimodal vision-language multi-agent pipeline that employs ReAct-driven reasoning to extract structured FEA specifications from heterogeneous inputs and (2) a verification-first code synthesis framework, incorporating robust self-debugging and fallback mechanisms to ensure executability and physical validity. We systematically evaluated the system across various engineering mechanics scenarios. The results demonstrate that VFEAgent achieves a high success rate in generating complete and physically valid simulations, outperforming LLM-based baseline methods in reliability and correctness. These findings validate the feasibility of automating the complete FEA workflow, highlighting the framework's potential to liberate engineers from tedious manual analysis.

infographic.key_insight: VFEAgent automates finite element analysis end-to-end from images and text by combining multimodal reasoning with verification-first code generation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The framework can reduce manual modeling effort and make FEA more accessible for engineering design workflows that require fast, reliable simulation.
method: Multimodal vision-language multi-agent pipeline parses heterogeneous inputs into structured FEA specifications.
ReAct-style reasoning guides task decomposition and decision-making across agents.
Verification-first code synthesis adds self-debugging and fallback mechanisms to ensure executable, physically valid simulations.
infographic.evidence_signal: The authors evaluated VFEAgent across multiple engineering mechanics scenarios and report higher success in generating complete, physically valid simulations than LLM-based baselines.
infographic.watch_item: Performance likely depends on the quality of input images/descriptions and robust handling of edge cases, so human review may still be needed for safety-critical analyses.
infographic.paper_title: VFEAgent: A Multimodal Agent Framework for End-to-End Automated Finite Element Analysis

title: Advances in polyconvex anisotropic hyperelasticity
authors: Dominik K. Klein, Karl A. Kalina, Rogelio Ortigosa, Jesús Martínez-Frutos, Markus Kästner, Oliver Weeger
abstract: A key challenge in material theory is the formulation of models that satisfy all common mechanical constitutive conditions while retaining sufficient flexibility. In this context, several important modeling aspects remain unresolved for polyconvex anisotropic hyperelasticity. We address some of these challenges and apply our results for physics-augmented neural network (PANN) constitutive modeling. The main contributions of this paper are as follows: (1) We propose a new polyconvex PANN constitutive model for anisotropic hyperelasticity based on triclinic invariants and group symmetrization. For finite symmetry groups, this model fulfills all common mechanical constitutive conditions a priori. (2) We propose a group symmetrization-based method for the construction of polyconvex invariants for finite symmetry groups. Based on this, we derive a new integrity basis for a tetragonal symmetry group and a new functional basis for a cubic symmetry group. To the best of our knowledge, these are the first polyconvex integrity or functional bases for symmetry groups characterized by structural tensors of order higher than two. (3) We provide an extensive introduction to the construction of polyconvex integrity and functional bases, which form the basis of polyconvex invariant-based constitutive models. We discuss polyconvex bases for triclinic, isotropic, transversely isotropic, monoclinic, rhombic, tetragonal, and cubic symmetry groups. (4) We benchmark the polyconvex PANN constitutive models with highly nonlinear homogenization data of cubic metamaterials.

infographic.key_insight: The paper introduces a polyconvex, symmetry-aware framework for anisotropic hyperelasticity that can satisfy standard mechanical constitutive requirements by construction.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables more reliable constitutive models for complex materials, especially for physics-augmented neural networks used in nonlinear solid mechanics.
method: Builds a new PANN constitutive model using triclinic invariants and group symmetrization.
Derives polyconvex invariants/bases for multiple symmetry classes, including new results for tetragonal and cubic groups.
Uses finite symmetry-group structure so constitutive conditions are enforced a priori.
infographic.evidence_signal: The proposed models are benchmarked against highly nonlinear homogenization data from cubic metamaterials.
infographic.watch_item: The approach relies on carefully chosen symmetry-group and invariant constructions, so extending it to more complex or imperfect material symmetries may require additional derivations.
infographic.paper_title: Advances in polyconvex anisotropic hyperelasticity

title: Interaction mechanics of acoustic cavitation with fibrin networks
authors: Aarushi Bhargava, Gaurav Gardi, Metin Sitti
abstract: Stiff and dense fibrin networks in chronic blood clots impede drug penetration and distribution into the clot core, limiting the efficacy of thrombolytic therapies. Acoustic cavitation of microbubbles is a promising strategy to enhance drug delivery in soft tissues. However, the interaction of these bubbles with stiff fibrin networks has yet to be investigated. Here, we show that ultrasound-driven bubbles undergoing stable periodic oscillations can penetrate and alter dense fibrin networks. The penetrated bubbles create three-dimensional paths that enable nanobeads (matrix transport markers) to infiltrate up to 200 $μ$m m deep into the mesh. Radial bubble oscillation is found to be the dominant forcing mechanism on fibrin fibers. Combining mechanical measurements with these observations reveals that the bubble radial stress is insufficient to break the fibrin fibers in a single cycle. Instead, repeated sub-fracture loading from bubble oscillations induce plastic deformation and damage accumulation with each cycle. This is evident from drastic dissipation losses and softening of the network seen over thousands of cycles. We further explored the softening of fibrin networks at a range of peak applied forces. At low force, the fibrin networks undergo a shakedown effect with initial softening, which is resistant to further damage after hundreds of cycles. At higher force, networks continue to soften without reaching a stable state, indicating progressive damage accumulation. These results show that cavitation can enhance matrix transport in dense fiber networks. The underlying physics is governed by the viscoplastic mechanics of bubble-fibrin interactions. These findings establish a mechanistic framework to design comprehensive treatment strategies for fibrotic aged clots.

infographic.key_insight: Ultrasound-driven cavitation bubbles can penetrate dense fibrin networks and, through repeated sub-fracture oscillatory loading, progressively soften and damage the mesh while creating transport pathways.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a mechanistic basis for using acoustic cavitation to improve drug penetration and thrombolysis in stiff, fibrotic clots.
method: Drive microbubbles with ultrasound in dense fibrin networks
Track bubble penetration, oscillation, and 3D path formation
Measure network mechanics over thousands of loading cycles
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports bubble-created 3D paths enabling nanobeads to infiltrate up to 200 µm deep, along with strong dissipation losses and softening over thousands of cycles.
infographic.watch_item: The bubble radial stress was insufficient to cause single-cycle fracture, so effectiveness depends on repeated loading and the resulting damage accumulation rather than immediate breakage.
infographic.paper_title: Interaction mechanics of acoustic cavitation with fibrin networks

title: Data-Driven Structural Health Monitoring of Short Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Polymer Composites via Multiphysics Phase-Field Simulation
authors: Behrouz Arash, Shadab Zakavati, Quan Wang, Timon Rabczuk
abstract: Short carbon fiber-reinforced polymer (SCFRP) composites exploit the intrinsic conductivity of the carbon fiber network for self-sensing, yet no predictive model couples their anisotropic, rate-dependent fracture to piezoresistive damage identification. This work presents a finite deformation multiphysics phase-field framework coupling a viscoelastic-viscoplastic constitutive model, an anisotropic crack resistance formulation, and a piezoresistive conductivity model. The three sub-problems are unified through the second-order fiber orientation tensor, which simultaneously defines fiber family directions, crack resistance anisotropy, and principal conduction paths of the carbon fiber network. A damage-coupled conductivity tensor captures both strain-driven geometric-kinematic resistance changes and irreversible network severance driven by the phase-field variable. The framework is coupled to an eight-electrode electrical impedance tomography configuration, and the normalized inter-electrode conductance ratios serve as inputs to a feedforward artificial neural network that infers normalized crack length and mechanical compliance without mechanical sensing. The network achieves R2 = 0.99 on held-out configurations, confirming generalization across the microstructure space. The framework establishes a physics-based, computationally efficient route for real-time structural health monitoring and inverse damage assessment in SCFRP composites.

infographic.key_insight: A unified multiphysics phase-field model links anisotropic, rate-dependent fracture and piezoresistive conductivity through fiber orientation, enabling damage inference in short carbon fiber composites.
infographic.engineering_relevance: It provides a physics-based route for real-time structural health monitoring of SCFRP parts without direct mechanical sensing.
method: Finite-deformation phase-field fracture coupled with viscoelastic-viscoplastic constitutive behavior.
Second-order fiber orientation tensor used to define crack resistance anisotropy and conduction pathways.
Eight-electrode EIT data fed to a neural network to predict normalized crack length and compliance.
infographic.evidence_signal: The feedforward neural network achieved R2 = 0.99 on held-out configurations, indicating strong generalization across microstructure variations.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on accurate orientation-tensor and conductivity-model calibration, so transfer to new composite layups or loading rates may require retraining or validation.
infographic.paper_title: Data-Driven Structural Health Monitoring of Short Carbon Fiber-Reinforced Polymer Composites via Multiphysics Phase-Field Simulation

title: Bistability of midpoint-fused arches with pinned-pinned boundary conditions
authors: Rajat Goswami, Safvan Palathingal
abstract: Arranging multiple arches in a circular pattern and fusing them at their midpoint yields a three-dimensional configuration that we refer to as midpoint-fused arches (MFA). This study investigates the structural bistability of MFA, i.e., their ability to admit two distinct, force-free stable equilibrium states. Starting from an as-fabricated, stress-free configuration, MFA can invert into a stressed, toggled state reminiscent of an umbrella's ribs. We develop an analytical model for the response of a pinned-pinned MFA subjected to a concentrated mid-span load by minimizing the total potential energy. Individual arches are treated as spatially deforming, and kinematic compatibility relations are derived at the fusion point to couple their deformations. Various deformation symmetries are then exploited to simplify the problem. We demonstrate the model's utility by characterizing the force-displacement response of a two-arch MFA, identifying distinct deformation pathways and discussing the pathway transitions that occur during toggling. In particular, we show how the structure switches between symmetric and asymmetric deformation modes as it moves between stable configurations. The generality of the framework is further established through analysis of a three-arch MFA, which exhibits richer coupled deformation behaviour. Nonlinear finite-element simulations and table-top experiments corroborate the analytical predictions, showing close agreement in both equilibrium states and the associated transition responses.

infographic.key_insight: Midpoint-fused arches can snap between two stable, force-free shapes, and the paper explains this bistability with an energy-based pinned-pinned model.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This provides a design framework for deployable or reconfigurable shell/arch structures that must toggle reliably between configurations.
method: Minimize total potential energy for pinned-pinned midpoint-fused arches under a concentrated mid-span load
Derive kinematic compatibility at the fusion point to couple multiple arches
Use symmetry assumptions to reduce the nonlinear deformation problem and analyze 2-arch and 3-arch cases
infographic.evidence_signal: The analytical predictions are validated against nonlinear finite-element simulations and table-top experiments, with close agreement in both equilibrium states and transition responses.
infographic.watch_item: The model depends on symmetry choices and ideal pinned-pinned boundary conditions, so real structures with manufacturing imperfections or boundary compliance may deviate.
infographic.paper_title: Bistability of midpoint-fused arches with pinned-pinned boundary conditions

title: Physics-constrained identification of graph-based thermal networks for spacecraft digital twins
authors: Luca Sosta, Carlo Ciancarelli, Leonardo Marini, Stefano Pagani, Francesco Regazzoni, Nicola Parolini
abstract: Reconstructing a thermal model capable of efficiently simulating the behavior of a spacecraft from sparse and localized temperature measurements remains a challenging task. To address this, we introduce a physically-constrained calibration framework for Lumped Parameter Thermal Models (LPTMs), formulated as a trajectory-based inverse problem for graph dynamical systems. The model reconstructs thermal dynamics directly from temperature measurements and known inputs, without relying on a priori parameter values derived from material properties or geometric assumptions. Physical admissibility is enforced at the parameterization level: positivity of nodal coefficients and symmetry of conductive interactions are imposed by construction. This guarantees stable dynamics and restricts the identification problem to a physically meaningful parameter space, improving conditioning without the need of additional regularization. The identification problem is addressed through trajectory matching, ensuring stable rollout over extended time horizons. The methodology is validated on synthetic datasets generated from high-fidelity finite element simulations under progressively complex forcing conditions. The calibrated LPTMs accurately reproduce long-term temperature evolution and exhibit robustness to measurement noise. The proposed framework provides a systematic approach to the calibration of reduced-order thermal models by combining physical structure with data-driven identification. The numerical results show a favorable balance between accuracy and computational efficiency, making the models suitable for integration in spacecraft thermal Digital Twin applications.

infographic.key_insight: A physics-constrained graph-based LPTM can be identified directly from sparse temperature measurements and inputs, yielding a stable and physically meaningful thermal surrogate for spacecraft digital twins.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach offers a practical reduced-order thermal model that balances accuracy and computational efficiency for spacecraft thermal analysis and long-horizon prediction.
method: Formulate thermal calibration as a trajectory-matching inverse problem on a graph dynamical system.
Enforce physical admissibility by construction: positive nodal coefficients and symmetric conductive couplings.
Fit model parameters using measured temperature trajectories and known inputs without relying on material- or geometry-derived priors.
infographic.evidence_signal: The method was validated on synthetic datasets from high-fidelity finite element simulations, and it remained accurate over long time horizons and robust to measurement noise.
infographic.watch_item: Performance is demonstrated on synthetic FEM-generated data, so transfer to real spacecraft telemetry may require careful handling of model mismatch and sensor sparsity.
infographic.paper_title: Physics-constrained identification of graph-based thermal networks for spacecraft digital twins

title: Preconditioned Discontinuous Galerkin Method for Elliptic Interface Problem on Unfitted Mesh with Reconstructed Discontinuous Approximation
authors: Ruo Li, Qicheng Liu, Fanyi Yang, Shuhai Zhao
abstract: In this paper, we develop an efficient preconditioned unfitted finite element method for the elliptic interface problem, based on the reconstructed discontinuous approximation. The approximation method for interface problems is originally proposed in [Li et al. SIAM J. Sci. Comput. 42(2), 2020], in which an arbitrarily high-order approximation space with one degree of freedom per element is constructed by solving local least squares fitting problems. The space can be applied within the cut discontinuous Galerkin framework, where the jump conditions across the interface are weakly enforced by the Nitsche's penalty method. In this work, the local least squares problem is modified by introducing appropriate constraints, which allows us to naturally ensure the stability near the interface by the reconstructed space, and further enables us to establish a norm equivalence between the high-order space and the lowest-order space. This equivalence property motivates us to construct a preconditioner from the piecewise constant space, and this preconditioning method is shown to be optimal in the sense that the upper bound of the condition number of the preconditioned system is independent of the mesh size, the coefficient and the interface location relative to the unfitted mesh. We also present the multigrid algorithms that serve as the inverse of the lowest-order system matrix. Numerical experiments in both two and three dimensions confirm the optimal convergence rates under error measurements and illustrate the efficiency and the robustness of the preconditioning method.

infographic.key_insight: A reconstructed discontinuous Galerkin formulation on unfitted meshes can be preconditioned using a lowest-order piecewise constant space, yielding mesh-, coefficient-, and interface-robust solver performance.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables accurate and efficient simulation of elliptic interface problems with complex geometries without requiring mesh conformity to the interface.
method: Build a high-order reconstructed discontinuous approximation from local least-squares fits with one DOF per element.
Modify the local reconstruction with constraints to improve stability near the interface and prove norm equivalence to the lowest-order space.
Use the piecewise constant space as a preconditioner, with multigrid acting as the inverse of the lowest-order system.
infographic.evidence_signal: Numerical experiments in 2D and 3D report optimal convergence and show the preconditioner remains effective independent of mesh size, coefficients, and interface position.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality of the local reconstruction and the multigrid solve for the lowest-order system, which may require careful implementation on highly cut meshes.
infographic.paper_title: Preconditioned Discontinuous Galerkin Method for Elliptic Interface Problem on Unfitted Mesh with Reconstructed Discontinuous Approximation

title: Active Continual Learning with Metaplastic Binary Bayesian Neural Networks
authors: Kellian Cottart, Théo Ballet, Djohan Bonnet, Damien Querlioz
abstract: Always-on edge systems must keep learning as conditions change under tight compute budgets and must detect unreliable predictions. Bayesian binary neural networks are attractive in this setting, but mean-field Bernoulli posteriors can saturate on long non-stationary streams, wiping out epistemic uncertainty and freezing plasticity. We propose BiMU, derived from a bounded-memory variational objective that balances stability, plasticity, and forgetting. BiMU combines a data term with controlled relaxation toward the prior and an uncertainty-dependent step size that prevents saturation and sustains informative uncertainty. This non-degenerate posterior enables fully online, buffer-free active querying via Monte Carlo disagreement, reducing label queries and backpropagation updates under imbalance. BiMU sustains learning and strong OOD detection on 1000-tasks Permuted-MNIST, and on OpenLORIS-Object achieves up to 32$\times$ label/update savings at matched accuracy under class imbalance and feature compression.

infographic.key_insight: BiMU keeps Bayesian binary neural networks plastic and uncertain over long non-stationary streams by using a bounded-memory variational update with uncertainty-aware step sizing.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables always-on edge learning systems to adapt online, query labels only when needed, and maintain reliable out-of-distribution detection under tight compute and memory budgets.
method: Combines the data likelihood with controlled relaxation toward the prior to balance stability, plasticity, and forgetting.
Uses an uncertainty-dependent step size to prevent posterior saturation and preserve epistemic uncertainty.
Supports fully online, buffer-free active querying via Monte Carlo disagreement to reduce label and backpropagation updates.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports strong results on 1000-task Permuted-MNIST and OpenLORIS-Object, including up to 32× savings in labels/updates at matched accuracy.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on careful control of the bounded-memory variational update and may be sensitive to imbalance, compression, and the quality of uncertainty estimates.
infographic.paper_title: Active Continual Learning with Metaplastic Binary Bayesian Neural Networks

title: A Variational Quantum Algorithm for Nonlinear Finite Element Analysis of Hyperelastic Materials
authors: Uditnarayan Kouskiya, Caglar Oskay
abstract: This manuscript explores a variational quantum formulation for nonlinear elasticity problems arising from hyperelastic material models, targeting near term noisy intermediate scale quantum (NISQ) devices. The approach leverages the potential energy structure of hyperelasticity and employs a hybrid quantum classical framework in which the energy functional is evaluated using parameterized quantum circuits and optimized through classical routines. To enable implementation on current quantum hardware, polynomial approximations of the nonlinear strain energy density are introduced, yielding a representation compatible with variational quantum algorithms. The methodology is demonstrated on a one dimensional NeoHookean material model using finite element discretizations with first and second order shape functions and nonhomogeneous boundary conditions. Numerical experiments investigate the influence of the polynomial approximation order on the accuracy and efficiency of the proposed approach, illustrating its feasibility for near term quantum devices.

infographic.key_insight: The paper reformulates nonlinear hyperelastic finite element analysis as a variational quantum optimization problem by approximating the strain energy with polynomials suitable for NISQ hardware.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This could provide a hybrid quantum-classical route for solving nonlinear elasticity problems in solid mechanics when conventional methods become computationally expensive.
method: Build a finite element discretization of the hyperelastic potential energy.
Replace the nonlinear strain energy density with a polynomial approximation compatible with parameterized quantum circuits.
Use a hybrid workflow where a quantum circuit evaluates the energy and a classical optimizer updates the parameters.
infographic.evidence_signal: The approach is demonstrated on a 1D NeoHookean material model with first- and second-order elements and nonhomogeneous boundary conditions, with experiments assessing accuracy and efficiency versus polynomial order.
infographic.watch_item: The method depends on polynomial approximation quality and is currently shown only on a simplified 1D test problem, so scalability and hardware noise effects remain open concerns.
infographic.paper_title: A Variational Quantum Algorithm for Nonlinear Finite Element Analysis of Hyperelastic Materials

title: PEFT-Arena: Understanding Parameter-Efficient Finetuning from a Stability-Plasticity Perspective
authors: Yangyi Huang, Ruotian Peng, Zeju Qiu, Jiale Kang, Yandong Wen, Bernhard Schölkopf
abstract: Parameter-efficient finetuning (PEFT) has become the standard approach for adapting large language models, yet evaluations largely emphasize downstream accuracy while overlooking the retention of pretrained capabilities. We argue that PEFT should be assessed through the stability-plasticity dilemma: the trade-off between target-task adaptation and resistance to forgetting. We introduce PEFT-Arena, a benchmark that jointly measures downstream performance and general capability retention. Across methods, we find distinct stability-plasticity profiles; under comparable parameter budgets, orthogonal finetuning achieves the most favorable Pareto frontier. To explain these differences, we analyze PEFT updates from two geometric perspectives. In weight space, spectral analysis reveals how parameterizations interact with the pretrained singular-value structure. In activation space, retention metrics show whether finetuning preserves or distorts general-capability representations, with forgetting linked to non-isometric representation distortion. Finally, an analysis shows that final SFT checkpoints often overshoot a better target-retention operating point. Inspired by this, we present case studies of a post-hoc improvement with path-wise rewinding.

infographic.key_insight: PEFT methods should be judged not only by task accuracy, but by how well they balance adaptation with retention of pretrained capabilities.
infographic.engineering_relevance: For large-model adaptation in computational science and engineering, this highlights which finetuning choices preserve general-purpose reliability while still meeting downstream task goals.
method: PEFT-Arena benchmark jointly measures downstream performance and capability retention.
Compares PEFT methods through stability-plasticity trade-offs under matched parameter budgets.
Uses spectral analysis in weight space and retention metrics in activation space to explain forgetting.
infographic.evidence_signal: Across methods and evaluation settings, the abstract reports distinct stability-plasticity profiles and identifies orthogonal finetuning as achieving the strongest Pareto frontier under comparable parameter budgets.
infographic.watch_item: Reported gains depend on the benchmark design and parameter budget, so results may not transfer unchanged to other model families or tasks.
infographic.paper_title: PEFT-Arena: Understanding Parameter-Efficient Finetuning from a Stability-Plasticity Perspective

title: Amorphous vs. Short-Range-Ordered Complexions: Consequences for Grain-Boundary-Mediated Plasticity in Nanocrystalline Al-Ni Alloys
authors: Frederic Sansoz, Eve-Audrey Picard
abstract: Amorphous grain-boundary (GB) complexions in thermally stable nanocrystalline alloys are commonly assumed to be structurally homogeneous, yet their disordered nature makes them susceptible to local short-range ordering (SRO). The influence of local SRO on GB-mediated plasticity mechanisms in such complexions remains poorly understood. This article employs large-scale Monte Carlo and molecular dynamics simulation to address this gap through simulations of nanocrystalline Al-Ni alloys at two Ni concentrations, 2 at.% and 4 at.%. Annealing at 913 K produces thick uniform amorphous intergranular film complexions, while annealing at 378 K produces semi-amorphous complexions containing FCC-type and BCC-type SRO. These two complexion states produce fundamentally different mechanical responses. Amorphous complexions act as dislocation sinks, suppressing shear localization and promoting homogeneous plasticity through shear transformation zones, but at the cost of lower strength. SRO complexions generate higher strength but also promote heterogeneous stress concentrations across the GB network, leading to intense shear localization regardless of Ni concentration. This contrast reflects a fundamental shift in governing mechanism, from shear-transformation-zone-controlled behavior in amorphous complexion alloys to GB-stress-heterogeneity-controlled behavior in SRO complexion alloys. These findings highlight the potential of complexion engineering to tailor the mechanical properties of nanocrystalline materials.

infographic.key_insight: Short-range order inside grain-boundary complexions fundamentally changes deformation in nanocrystalline Al-Ni: amorphous films favor homogeneous plasticity via shear-transformation zones, while SRO complexions raise strength but trigger stress heterogeneity and shear localization.
infographic.engineering_relevance: Complexion engineering can be used to tune the strength-ductility balance in nanocrystalline alloys by controlling whether grain boundaries are amorphous or short-range ordered.
method: Large-scale Monte Carlo simulations to generate complexion states at two Ni levels (2 at.% and 4 at.%).
Molecular dynamics simulations to probe mechanical response after annealing at 913 K vs. 378 K.
Comparison of amorphous intergranular films with semi-amorphous FCC/BCC SRO complexions.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports two composition cases and two annealing conditions, showing a consistent shift from homogeneous plasticity in amorphous complexions to localized shear in SRO complexions.
infographic.watch_item: The conclusions are simulation-based for specific Al-Ni compositions and annealing states, so transferability to other alloy systems or processing windows should be validated experimentally.
infographic.paper_title: Amorphous vs. Short-Range-Ordered Complexions: Consequences for Grain-Boundary-Mediated Plasticity in Nanocrystalline Al-Ni Alloys

title: Towards end-to-end LLM-based censoring-aware survival analysis
authors: Yishu Wei, Hexin Dong, Yi Lin, Jiahe Qian, Yi Liu, Yifan Peng
abstract: Objective: Survival analysis is central to medical prediction, yet large language models (LLMs) are rarely used as end-to-end survival models because censoring prevents straightforward supervised fine-tuning. Here we present LLMSurvival, a framework that enables censoring-aware survival analysis with unmodified LLMs operating directly on tabular clinical data. Materials and Methods: LLMSurvival reformulates time-to-event prediction as pairwise ranking among comparable subjects, and derives test-time risk by aggregating comparisons against anchor individuals from the training cohort. Results: Across two clinical tasks (ICU mortality prediction in MIMIC-IV and fragility fracture prediction in a NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell Medicine cohort), LLMSurvival improves overall concordance over Cox proportional hazards modeling by 3.1% for ICU mortality and 0.5% for fracture risk, 2.1% on average for ICU mortality and 2.8% for fracture risk over three established deep learning survival models. Discussion: The results show that survival modeling with censoring can be made compatible with LLM fine-tuning through comparison-based reformulation. The framework demonstrates high portability and superior performance over expert curated scores like SAPS-II and FRAX scores across diverse clinical context. Furthermore, the framework supports local deployment, as compact, publicly available base models provide sufficient performance. Conclusion: The LLMSurvival framework serves as a proof of concept for an integrated, censoring-conscious approach to survival analysis via LLMs.

infographic.key_insight: LLMSurvival turns censored survival prediction into a pairwise ranking problem, enabling unmodified LLMs to learn end-to-end risk models from tabular clinical data.
infographic.engineering_relevance: The approach offers a portable, local-deployable alternative to traditional survival models and expert scores for failure/risk prediction in real-world engineering and medical decision settings.
method: Reformulate time-to-event prediction as comparisons between comparable subjects.
Fine-tune standard LLMs on tabular data using censoring-aware pairwise ranking.
Estimate test-time risk by aggregating comparisons against anchor patients from training.
infographic.evidence_signal: Evaluated on ICU mortality (MIMIC-IV) and fragility fracture prediction (NewYork-Presbyterian/Weill Cornell), with improved concordance over Cox models and established deep survival baselines.
infographic.watch_item: Performance depends on the quality and coverage of anchor comparisons, so cohort shift or sparse comparable pairs could reduce reliability.
infographic.paper_title: Towards end-to-end LLM-based censoring-aware survival analysis

title: Microstructure-Aware Deep Learning Bridges Atomistics to Macroscale for Shock-to-Detonation Prediction
authors: Simon Gonzalez-Zapata, Aidan Pantoya, Chunyu Li, Marisol Koslowski, Alejandro Strachan
abstract: The shock-to-detonation transition in energetic materials is governed by coupled processes spanning Angstroms to millimeters and femtoseconds to microseconds, where traditional multiscale models fail due to the lack of scale separation. We address this grand challenge by directly bridging large-scale molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with continuum finite-element (FE) models using MISTnetX, a convolutional deep neural network. Trained on MD simulations of shock propagation through complex microstructures, MISTnetX captures shock-microstructure interactions, hotspot formation, and the transition to deflagration, supplying critical sub-grid information to FE simulations of mechanics, shocks, thermal transport, and chemistry. Applied to a synthetic but realistic nanostructured plastic-bonded RDX composite, MISTnetX enables parameter-free prediction of the full run-to-detonation transition.

infographic.key_insight: A microstructure-aware convolutional neural network can bridge atomistic MD and continuum FE to predict shock-to-detonation behavior in energetic composites without relying on traditional scale separation.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This enables parameter-free, multiscale prediction of hotspot formation, deflagration, and run-to-detonation in nanostructured energetic materials, improving design and safety analysis.
method: Train MISTnetX on large-scale molecular dynamics shock simulations with complex microstructures.
Use the network to provide sub-grid shock–microstructure, thermal, and chemistry information to continuum finite-element models.
Apply the coupled MD-to-FE workflow to a synthetic nanostructured plastic-bonded RDX composite.
infographic.evidence_signal: The abstract reports that MISTnetX was trained on MD shock-propagation data and successfully applied to predict the full run-to-detonation transition in a realistic synthetic composite.
infographic.watch_item: The demonstrated application is on a synthetic but realistic RDX composite, so broader validation across different energetic formulations and microstructure classes is still needed.
infographic.paper_title: Microstructure-Aware Deep Learning Bridges Atomistics to Macroscale for Shock-to-Detonation Prediction

title: Revisiting Bruck: Phase-Efficient All-to-All Communication in Reconfigurable Networks
authors: Anton Juerss, Stefan Schmid
abstract: All-to-All communication is a key performance bottleneck for distributed machine learning (ML) and high-performance computing (HPC) workloads, where dense traffic increasingly stresses scale-up interconnects. While these ML and HPC workloads have driven unprecedented infrastructure demand, optical reconfigurable networks (ORNs) offer a promising path forward. By adapting the physical topology to the active workload, they improve communication cost and bandwidth utilization. However, their benefit is critically contingent on whether the collective consists of structured phases that can be served by sparse and reusable topology states. In this paper, we revisit Bruck's All-to-All implementation and demonstrate the benefits of topology optimization in which both communication pattern and reconfiguration strategy are co-designed. We present ReTri, a bidirectional All-to-All schedule for ORNs. ReTri uses balanced ternary block propagation to complete All-to-All in $\lceil \log_3 n\rceil$ phases. The induced reconfiguration strategy from ReTri's pairwise bidirectional exchanges allow reconfiguration delays to be amortized across multiple phases. Preliminary simulations show that ReTri improves completion time by up to $10\times$ over static All-to-All, even for millisecond-scale reconfiguration delays, and improving reconfigurable Bruck by up to $2.1\times$.

infographic.key_insight: ReTri co-designs all-to-all communication and optical network reconfiguration so the collective finishes in only ⌈log3 n⌉ phases with reusable topology states.
infographic.engineering_relevance: This can substantially reduce completion time and improve bandwidth utilization for distributed ML and HPC systems that are bottlenecked by dense all-to-all traffic.
method: Uses balanced ternary block propagation for bidirectional all-to-all communication.
Schedules pairwise exchanges so reconfiguration delays are amortized across multiple phases.
Revisits Bruck’s algorithm and adapts it to reconfigurable optical networks.
infographic.evidence_signal: Preliminary simulations report up to 10× faster completion than static all-to-all and up to 2.1× improvement over reconfigurable Bruck, even with millisecond-scale reconfiguration delays.
infographic.watch_item: Benefits depend on structured phased traffic and a reconfiguration model that can be amortized; performance may drop for less regular workloads or higher switching overheads.
infographic.paper_title: Revisiting Bruck: Phase-Efficient All-to-All Communication in Reconfigurable Networks
